stem: How many different equivalence relations on a set of [[n]] elements have exactly two equivalence classes? Equivalently, in how many ways can the set be split into two nonempty subsets (where the two subsets are interchangeable)?
solution: An equivalence relation corresponds to a partition. Splitting the [[n]] elements into an ordered pair of nonempty subsets can be done in \( 2^{[[n]]} - 2 \) ways; since the two classes are interchangeable, divide by 2 to get \( 2^{[[n]]-1} - 1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by 2 for the interchangeable classes (it counts ordered assignments minus one).
This forgets to remove the case where one subset is empty.
This counts ordered assignments into two nonempty labeled classes (surjections onto 2 labels).
This counts ways to choose a 2-element subset, not a 2-class partition.
This subtracts one too many.
This uses the number of elements minus one rather than a power of two.

stem: A group of [[n]] people is split into two DISTINGUISHABLE teams, Team A and Team B, so that both teams are nonempty. In how many ways can this be done?
solution: Each person independently joins Team A or Team B, giving \( 2^{[[n]]} \) assignments; remove the 2 assignments where one team is empty: \( 2^{[[n]]} - 2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the count when the two teams are interchangeable (unlabeled), which is half as many.
This forgets to exclude the two assignments where one team is empty.
This excludes only one of the two all-in-one-team assignments.
This is the count for a set with one fewer person.
This doubles the remaining-people count instead of using an exponent.
This multiplies the group size by one less than itself instead of exponentiating.

stem: A set with [[n]] elements is partitioned into two classes: one class of exactly [[k]] elements and a second class with the remaining elements. In how many ways can this partition be formed (i.e., in how many ways can the [[k]]-element class be chosen)?
solution: Since the two class sizes [[k]] and \( [[n]]-[[k]] \) differ, choosing which [[k]] elements form the smaller-labeled class determines the whole partition with no double-counting: \( \binom{[[n]]}{[[k]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This counts ordered selections (permutations) instead of an unordered choice of class members.
This wrongly halves the count as if the two classes could be confused, but their sizes differ so there is no overcount.
This is the total number of subsets of the whole set, ignoring the required class size.
This subtracts one for no valid reason.
This shifts both parameters down by one (a Pascal's-rule slip).
This is the size of the complementary class, not a count of partitions.

stem: In how many ways can a set of [[n]] elements be partitioned into three nonempty, unlabeled subsets (equivalently, how many equivalence relations on the set have exactly three equivalence classes)?
solution: The number of surjections onto 3 labeled classes is \( 3^{[[n]]} - 3\cdot2^{[[n]]} + 3 \) by inclusion-exclusion; since the three classes are unlabeled, divide by \( 3! = 6 \): \( \dfrac{3^{[[n]]} - 3\cdot2^{[[n]]} + 3}{6} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to divide by \( 3! \) to remove the labeling of the three classes.
This divides by 2 instead of \( 3! = 6 \).
This is the two-class partition formula, using the wrong number of classes.
This counts ALL functions onto 3 labels, without the inclusion-exclusion correction for emptiness.
This divides by 3 instead of \( 3! = 6 \).
This counts ways to choose a 3-element subset, not a 3-class partition.

stem: In how many ways can a set of [[n]] elements be partitioned into pairs, so that every element belongs to exactly one (unordered) pair?
solution: Arrange all [[n]] elements in a row ([[n]]! ways); this overcounts each matching by \( 2^{[[half]]} \) (order within each pair doesn't matter) and by \( [[half]]! \) (order of the pairs doesn't matter): \( \dfrac{[[n]]!}{2^{[[half]]}\,[[half]]!} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This only corrects for the order of the pairs, forgetting the order within each pair.
This treats the pairing as if it split into two equal labeled halves rather than \( [[half]] \) unordered pairs.
This over-corrects, dividing by \( 2^{[[n]]} \) instead of \( 2^{[[half]]} \).
This is a plain binomial coefficient, ignoring the internal structure of the pairing.
This only accounts for ordering the pairs, ignoring the arrangement of elements within them.
This is the denominator alone, not the full quotient.

stem: Define a relation \( \sim \) on the set \( \{1, 2, \dots, [[n]]\} \) by \( a \sim b \) if and only if \( a \) and \( b \) leave the same remainder when divided by [[k]]. This is an equivalence relation. How many equivalence classes does it have?
solution: The possible remainders when dividing by [[k]] are \( 0, 1, \dots, [[k]]-1 \), and since \( [[n]] \ge [[k]] \), every remainder actually occurs among \( 1, \dots, [[n]] \). So there are exactly [[k]] equivalence classes.
distractor_rationales: This confuses the number of elements with the number of classes.
This subtracts the modulus from the set size instead of recognizing the class count is just the modulus.
This is one less than the true number of possible remainders.
This is one more than the true number of possible remainders.
This estimates how many FULL cycles of remainders fit, rather than how many distinct remainders exist.
This rounds up the number of cycles instead of counting distinct remainders.

stem: Define a relation \( \sim \) on the set \( \{1, 2, \dots, [[n]]\} \) by \( a \sim b \) if and only if \( a \) and \( b \) leave the same remainder when divided by [[k]]. How many integers in \( \{1, \dots, [[n]]\} \) are equivalent to [[r]] under \( \sim \) (that is, how many elements share [[r]]'s equivalence class, including [[r]] itself)?
solution: The elements equivalent to [[r]] are \( [[r]], [[r]]+[[k]], [[r]]+2\cdot[[k]], \dots \) up to [[n]]; there are \( \left\lfloor \dfrac{[[n]]-[[r]]}{[[k]]} \right\rfloor + 1 = [[answer]] \) of them.
distractor_rationales: This ignores the offset \( r \) and just counts multiples of \( k \) up to \( n \).
This confuses the class SIZE with the number of classes (the modulus itself).
This confuses the class size with the representative element \( r \) itself.
This forgets to divide by the modulus \( k \).
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by one from the correct count.
This is off by two from the correct count.
